topic: A person learns their childhood bully became a therapist
raw_output:
[story 1]

The scent of old leather and faint lavender always hit me first when I walked into Dr. Elias Thorne's office. It was a stark contrast to the echoing hallways of middle school, where Elias had been a constant, unwelcome fixture. My hands, clasped tightly in my lap, felt clammy as I waited for him to call my name. When he finally looked up, his eyes—the same sharp, assessing grey eyes that used to narrow in judgment—held a strange, almost gentle focus. A tremor ran through my shoulders, a physical reaction I hadn't felt in years. "Come in, Clara," he said, his voice modulated, smooth as polished stone. I shuffled forward, my gaze fixed on the worn rug beneath my feet, trying to anchor myself against the sudden, overwhelming feeling of being exposed.

[story 2]

I kept catching glimpses of him in the news—an article about his groundbreaking work on trauma recovery. It was impossible. Elias Vance, the boy who once delighted in tripping me in the lunch line, was now a respected figure in the mental health field. A tightness coiled low in my stomach every time I saw his name. Today, I managed to secure an appointment. The waiting room felt suffocatingly quiet, amplifying the frantic rhythm of my pulse drumming against my ribs. When the door finally opened, and he stood there, impeccably dressed, the familiar surge of resentment warred violently with a profound, unsettling confusion. He offered a small, almost imperceptible nod, and the sheer incongruity of the scene made me want to simply retreat into the nearest shadow.

[story 3]

My palms were slick against the cool metal of the examination table. I stared at the ceiling tiles, tracing patterns in the dust motes dancing in the afternoon light. This room, so sterile and calm, felt like a trap. When Elias finally spoke, his tone was measured, devoid of any mockery I remembered from childhood. "Tell me what's been weighing on you today, Clara," he prompted. The request itself felt monumental, a demand for vulnerability I wasn't sure I possessed. My throat constricted, and a single, involuntary hitch escaped as I struggled to gather the words that felt too heavy, too true, to voice. I swallowed hard, the effort tasting like ash.
